description.s: Problem

Item search (Ctrl+K, Item Search view) supports filtering via a bespoke mini-language (type:, tag:, name:, title:, #shorthand, negation with -). Results always sort alphabetically. There is no way to sort by recency, filter by date, or compose queries beyond what the fixed syntax supports. Adding more capabilities means inventing more syntax — but we already have a programming language.
Proposal: Hob expressions as search predicates

When the search input starts with (, treat it as a Hob predicate expression. The search box macro-expands it into a complete Hob program that filters and sorts all items, then evaluates it in one interpreter call.
User writes
(type? "note")
Macro expands to
(filter (fn [i] (type? i "note")) (get-all))
With sort

;; user writes
(type? "note") (sort :modified)

;; expands to
(sort-by :modified :desc
  (filter (fn [i] (type? i "note")) (get-all)))
Compound predicates

;; user writes
(and (type? "note") (tagged? "todo") (not (tagged? "done")))

;; expands to
(filter (fn [i]
  (and (type? i "note") (tagged? i "todo") (not (tagged? i "done"))))
  (get-all))
Macro expansion rules
The search box applies a macro before evaluation. The macro:
Splits input on top-level expression boundaries. If there are two expressions and the second starts with (sort ...), extracts the sort directive
Walks the first (predicate) expression tree. For each call to a registered predicate function (type?, tagged?, etc.) that is missing its item argument, inserts i as the first argument
Wraps the result in (fn [i] ...) to produce a predicate function
Wraps in (filter pred (get-all)) and optionally (sort-by field :desc ...)

The macro needs a registry of predicate functions and their arities so it knows which calls are "missing" the item argument. This is a fixed, small set. For ad-hoc expressions, the user can reference i explicitly: (> (.-modified i) 1710000000000).
Predicate library
New stdlib or search-context functions. All take item as first argument.

(type? item "note")          ;; item.type matches type name or ID
(tagged? item "todo")        ;; item has tag by name or ID
(name? item "foo")           ;; item.name contains substring
(modified-after? item ts)    ;; item.modified > timestamp
(modified-before? item ts)   ;; item.modified < timestamp
(has-field? item :code)      ;; item.content has field
type? and tagged? resolve names to IDs (like the current search does), so users write human-readable names, not GUIDs.
Interaction model: Enter to evaluate
All search — both plain text and Hob — evaluates on Enter, not on every keystroke. This is a change from the current debounce model, motivated by two problems:
The current debounce sometimes triggers mid-typing, and when results render the trailing keystrokes are lost — the user ends up with the wrong search text.
For Hob expressions, debounce or balanced-paren detection can't tell whether the user intends to add a (sort ...) expression after the predicate. Enter makes intent explicit.
Behaviour:
Type freely
— no evaluation while typing.
Enter
— evaluate. If input starts with (, macro-expand and run as Hob. Otherwise, run as text/filter search (existing mini-syntax still supported).
Empty input
— show starred items (as today, on load).
The existing name:/type:/tag: syntax remains supported for quick interactive use. It is not replaced — it coexists. The Hob mode is for queries that go beyond what the mini-syntax can express.
Error handling

If a balanced Hob expression fails to evaluate (typo, unknown symbol, wrong arity, etc.), display the Hob error message inline in the results area — where "Searching..." or results normally appear. No results are shown. This gives the user immediate feedback to fix their query.
Alternatives considered and rejected:
Fall back to text search
— rejected because (type? "note") with a typo would silently become a text search for the literal string, returning random items that happen to contain those characters. Confusing.
Show nothing
— rejected because the user gets no signal about what went wrong. Showing the error is more helpful.
Performance
Benchmarked against 383 items (full database) and 2298 items (6x duplication):

383 items:
  JS filter:       0ms
  Hob per-item:   16ms  (JS loop calling Hob predicate per item)
  Hob all-in:    14ms  (entire filter inside Hob)

2298 items:
  JS filter:       0ms
  Hob per-item:   62ms  (JS↔Hob boundary crossing dominates)
  Hob all-in:      8ms  (single interpreter call)

Key finding: the JS↔Hob boundary crossing per item is the bottleneck, not Hob evaluation itself. Running the entire filter inside Hob (one boundary crossing) is fast — 8ms for 2298 items. The macro must therefore produce a complete Hob program, not a predicate function called from JS per item.
Sort

Sort is expressed as a second expression after the predicate. The search box splits the input on top-level expression boundaries: the first is the filter predicate, the second (optional) is the sort directive.

(type? "note") (sort :modified)           ;; find notes, most recent first
(tagged? "todo") (sort :name :asc)        ;; find todos, alphabetical
(and (type? "note") (tagged? "todo"))      ;; no sort — defaults to :modified :desc

This reads naturally left-to-right: "find items of this type, and sort them this way." Since evaluation is triggered by Enter (not balanced-paren detection), there is no ambiguity about whether the user intends to add a sort expression — Enter makes intent explicit.
If no sort is specified, default is :modified :desc (most recent first).
Implementation steps
Add predicate functions (type?, tagged?, etc.) to the Hob stdlib or as a search-context library
Implement the macro expander: sort peeling, predicate registry, item-arg insertion, filter/get-all wrapping
Add balanced-paren detection to the search input handler
Wire into item-search-lib: when input starts with (, macro-expand and evaluate via hob-interpreter, return results
Add sort:modified / sort:recent to the existing mini-syntax as a quick win (independent of Hob work)
Design rationale
No new mini-language
: Hob is already the system's programming language. Query power comes from composing the same functions used everywhere else.
Dogfoods Hob
: pushes Hob toward the "obvious choice for user programming" north star.
No ceiling
: any query expressible in Hob is available. Saved searches are Hob items — first-class, composable, shareable.
Macro keeps it terse
: the user writes (type? "note"), not (filter (fn [i] ...) (get-all)). The search context justifies the macro — it is unambiguous that the expression is a predicate over items.
Performance validated
: all-in-Hob evaluation is fast enough (<16ms for full database). Balanced-paren triggering avoids per-keystroke evaluation.

— current search implementation
